module.title: Rates and evidence
module.subtitle: Building and defending the financial case for a credit hire claim

learning_objectives.text: Explain how CHO rates are set and how they differ from basic hire rates (BHR)
Identify and evaluate the types of evidence used to establish or challenge the applicable rate
Assess impecuniosity evidence — what is required, what is sufficient, and what is disproportionate to demand
Explain the role of digital bank statements and the controversy surrounding their use as impecuniosity evidence
Recognise the tactics used by insurers to attack the evidence base of a credit hire claim and explain the response to each
Apply the proportionality principle to disclosure demands in lower-value credit hire claims

sections.title: How CHO rates are constructed
sections.concept: A credit hire rate is not simply a hire rate — it includes a credit element reflecting the CHO's cost of carrying the debt until the claim is settled. This credit element is recoverable only where the claimant needed it (i.e. could not have hired at spot rate). Understanding how CHO rates are built helps practitioners explain and justify them, and identify where an insurer's challenge has genuine merit vs where it is tactical.
content_points: Components of a CHO rate: base vehicle cost, fleet depreciation, insurance, administration, and the credit element (cost of carrying unpaid debt)
The credit element: reflects the CHO's working capital cost of providing vehicles on deferred payment terms; typically 15-30% above spot hire
Why CHO rates vary: market, vehicle class, geography, and the CHO's own cost structure all influence the rate
Spot hire vs CHO rates: spot hire companies (Enterprise, Hertz, Arnold Clark) charge on payment; they have no credit risk and therefore no credit element
The BHR comparator: insurers produce Basic Hire Rate evidence — surveys of what spot hire companies charge for a comparable vehicle in the local market
GTA rates vs open market rates: GTA rates are negotiated compromises; open market rates (CHO vs BHR) may diverge significantly for specialist vehicles
The rate as a snapshot: rates must reflect the market at the time of hire, not at the time of settlement

scenario.text: A CHO charges £85/day for a mid-size family car. The insurer produces a BHR survey showing that Enterprise, Hertz, and Arnold Clark all offer comparable vehicles for between £38-£52/day in the same city. The insurer argues only £52/day is recoverable. The claimant is impecunious. Analyse the rate dispute.
teaching_points: If the claimant is impecunious under Lagden, the full £85/day CHO rate is recoverable
BHR evidence is only relevant where the claimant is NOT impecunious
The learner must first establish impecuniosity before engaging with the rate challenge
If impecuniosity is not established, the BHR survey becomes the key battleground and £52/day is a defensible ceiling
The quality of the BHR survey matters — same area, same vehicle class, same time of hire

knowledge_check.question: A BHR survey produced by the insurer shows spot hire rates of £40-55/day. The CHO charged £78/day. The claimant is impecunious under Lagden v O'Brien. What is the rate that is most likely recoverable?

options.text: £40/day — the cheapest available spot rate
£55/day — the highest spot rate in the survey
£78/day — the full CHO rate, because the claimant's impecuniosity means they needed the credit element
options.explanation: Where the claimant is genuinely impecunious, the full CHO rate is recoverable regardless of what spot rates were available. BHR evidence is irrelevant for an impecunious claimant.

options.text: The average of the BHR survey rates

sections.title: BHR evidence: surveys, sources and challenges
sections.concept: Basic Hire Rate (BHR) evidence is the primary tool used by insurers to argue that a lower rate should apply where the claimant is not impecunious. The quality, methodology and timeliness of BHR evidence is frequently disputed. Practitioners must be able to assess BHR evidence critically — identifying where it is reliable, where it is flawed, and how to challenge it effectively.
content_points: What BHR evidence should show: the rate for a comparable vehicle, in the same geographic market, at the time of hire, from a mainstream rental company. Only where no mainstream supplier can provide a comparable vehicle should evidence from a reputable local company be considered
Common BHR sources: insurers use independent rate surveys, direct quotes from mainstream rental companies, or internal rate databases
Flaws in BHR evidence: wrong vehicle class, wrong geography, wrong hire period (daily vs weekly rates differ), rates obtained after the hire rather than at the time of hire

Timing of rate evidence: rates obtained for a hire start date several days before the hire actually starts are flawed. The closer the rate is obtained to the actual start of hire, the higher the rate will generally be — rates quoted days in advance do not reflect the true cost of hiring at short notice
The 'like-for-like' issue in BHR evidence: a survey for a 'mid-size car' may not reflect a vehicle comparable to a specific prestige or specialist model
Online quote evidence: insurers sometimes produce online quotes from rental websites; these may not reflect actual availability or walk-in rates
Challenging BHR evidence: the CHO should be prepared to produce counter-evidence — quotes for the same vehicle, same period, from the same market, obtained as close to the hire start date as possible

scenario.text: The insurer produces a BHR survey for a Ford Transit van hire showing rates of £55-75/day. The claimant hired a Transit from the CHO at £110/day. The survey was obtained 6 weeks after the hire ended. The rates are from national rental companies. The claimant's van was hired in a rural area of Scotland where two of the three companies surveyed have no local branch. Identify the weaknesses in the BHR evidence.
teaching_points: Timing: obtained 6 weeks post-hire — rates may have changed
Geography: rural Scotland — national companies may have no local presence; rates reflect urban markets
Availability: if the surveyed companies have no branch in the area, their quoted rates are theoretical
The learner should identify all three weaknesses and understand they go to the weight of the evidence
The CHO should produce counter-evidence: what rates were actually available locally at the time

knowledge_check.question: Which of the following most undermines the reliability of a BHR survey produced by the insurer?

options.text: The survey was produced by an independent research company
The survey quotes rates for the same vehicle class but from companies that had no branch within 40 miles of where the claimant actually hired
options.explanation: Geographic irrelevance is a fundamental flaw — if the quoted companies could not actually provide a vehicle to the claimant, their rates are not true comparators.

options.text: The insurer paid for the survey
The survey shows rates lower than the CHO rate

sections.title: Impecuniosity evidence: what is needed and what is enough
sections.concept: Proving impecuniosity requires the claimant to show they could not reasonably have afforded spot hire at the time of the accident. There is no fixed threshold but courts look at the whole picture: income, savings, credit availability, and whether paying upfront would have caused genuine hardship. The evidence must be proportionate to the value of the claim — demanding six months of bank statements for a £400 rate dispute is oppressive. The Forum has tracked the growing use of digital bank statement technology as a less intrusive solution.
content_points: What impecuniosity means in practice: the claimant could not have hired at the spot rate from their own resources at the time of the accident
The evidence typically sought: bank statements, payslips, credit card statements, overdraft limits, evidence of benefits
The proportionality requirement: evidence demands must be proportionate to the value at stake — a £500 rate differential does not justify demanding three months of bank statements
Income is not the only factor: a claimant earning £30,000 with substantial debts and outgoings may be impecunious; a claimant earning £18,000 with savings may not be
Credit availability: a claimant with an unused credit card with a £2,000 limit is harder to argue as impecunious; one with a maxed-out card is not
Self-certification: some CHOs ask claimants to self-certify their financial position; courts give this limited weight without supporting documentary evidence
The digital bank statement solution: technology now allows claimants to share a read-only view of relevant bank data without producing physical statements — reducing delay and intrusion
The threshold question: courts have not set a fixed income or savings figure; the assessment is holistic and fact-specific

scenario.text: Aisha is a home care worker earning £13.50/hour working 25 hours per week (approximately £17,550/year). She has a current account with an average balance of £180 between paydays. She has a credit card with a £500 limit that is consistently at 85-90% utilisation. She has no savings. Her car is written off. The CHO rate is £65/day; BHR is £30/day. The insurer demands: 6 months of bank statements, 3 months of payslips, a credit reference report, and a sworn statement of means. Advise.
teaching_points: Aisha's financial profile strongly supports impecuniosity
The insurer's demand is disproportionate — this is a relatively modest rate differential
3 months of bank statements and 1-2 payslips would likely be sufficient
A credit reference report and sworn statement are excessive for this value
The learner should advise providing proportionate evidence and challenging the scope of the demand
The Forum has reported that courts have criticised oppressive disclosure demands in credit hire cases

knowledge_check.question: A claimant self-certifies that they are impecunious by signing a form stating they could not afford spot hire. The insurer challenges. How much weight will a court likely give to the self-certification alone?

options.text: Full weight — the claimant has confirmed it under their own hand
Limited weight — self-certification without supporting documentary evidence is unlikely to be sufficient; courts expect corroborating financial evidence
options.explanation: Self-certification is a starting point, not a conclusion. Courts expect it to be supported by bank statements, payslips, or other objective evidence of the claimant's financial position.

options.text: No weight — impecuniosity can only be proved by bank statements
Full weight, unless the insurer produces evidence that the claimant is not impecunious

sections.title: Digital bank statements and the disclosure battleground
sections.concept: The use of digital bank statement technology — platforms that allow a claimant to share a read-only, categorised view of their bank transactions — has emerged as a practical solution to the impecuniosity evidence debate. It reduces delay (no need to request paper statements), reduces intrusion (the claimant controls what is shared), and provides objective data. However, it remains contested. The Forum has covered the evolution of this issue extensively, including the insurer push for more extensive financial disclosure and the courts' increasingly critical response.
content_points: What digital bank statement technology does: connects directly to the claimant's bank via open banking, categorises transactions, and produces a summary or detailed view without sharing account credentials
The advantage for CHOs: faster impecuniosity evidence, less friction for claimants, more objective than self-certification
The advantage for claimants: less intrusive than producing paper statements, claimant controls the scope of sharing
Insurer acceptance: some insurers now accept digital bank statement evidence; others still demand paper statements
The scope dispute: insurers often demand 3-6 months of full statements; courts have indicated that a shorter period may suffice where the financial position is clear
What the statements should show: average balances, credit card limits and utilisation, regular income and outgoings, absence of significant savings
The proportionality principle in CPR: courts expect disclosure requests to be proportionate to the issues and the value of the claim; disproportionate demands may result in adverse costs orders

scenario.text: A CHO uses a digital bank statement platform. The claimant completes the process within 2 hours of instruction, sharing 2 months of current account data. The platform report shows an average balance of £95, consistent income of £1,200/month, no savings account, and a credit card with £150 available. The insurer says they will not accept the digital statement and demand 6 months of original paper statements and a credit reference report. How should the CHO respond?
teaching_points: The digital statement provides objective, real-time evidence of the claimant's position
The insurer's refusal to accept it should be challenged — it is irrational if the data is accurate
The CHO should assert that the digital evidence is sufficient and proportionate
If litigation follows, the CHO should flag the proportionality issue to the court
On these facts — £95 average balance, no savings, £150 credit — impecuniosity is strongly made out regardless of format

knowledge_check.question: Which of the following best describes the proportionality principle as applied to impecuniosity disclosure demands?

options.text: The insurer can demand as much evidence as they wish because proving impecuniosity is the claimant's obligation
Evidence demands must be proportionate to the value of the rate differential at stake — a small rate difference does not justify extensive financial disclosure
options.explanation: CPR proportionality applies to disclosure in credit hire as in all civil litigation. Courts have criticised excessive demands and may penalise them in costs.

options.text: Only digital bank statements are proportionate — paper statements cannot be demanded
Proportionality only applies in cases worth less than £10,000

sections.title: Insurer tactics: recognising and responding to standard challenges
sections.concept: Insurers use a range of standard tactics to challenge credit hire claims — many of which arrive as template letters regardless of the specific facts. Experienced practitioners learn to distinguish genuine challenges (where the insurer has identified a real weakness) from volume tactics (where the challenge is sent as a matter of policy). Understanding the playbook allows the CHO to respond efficiently, prioritise issues, and avoid wasting resources on spurious challenges.
content_points: The template letter: many insurers send standard challenge letters within 24-48 hours of hire notification; these rarely engage with the specific facts and should be treated as tactical until proven otherwise
Common challenge types: rate challenge (BHR only), period challenge (hire is too long), need challenge (claimant did not need a vehicle), impecuniosity challenge (claimant is not impecunious)
The 'mitigation offer': the insurer offers their own vehicle at their own rate; the claimant is not obliged to accept but the offer is relevant to what is reasonable
The notification delay argument: where a CHO notifies the insurer late, the insurer may argue they had no opportunity to mitigate; late notification weakens but does not necessarily defeat the claim
The 'you should have used public transport' argument: typically spurious unless the claimant's vehicle use was genuinely limited to journeys easily replaceable by public transport
The fraud flag: insurers occasionally flag claims as potentially fraudulent; this changes the dynamic significantly and requires careful handling
The Part 36 offer: in litigation, insurers use Part 36 offers to manage costs risk; understanding when to accept and when to press on is a key practitioner skill
Responding effectively: acknowledge the challenge, address each point factually, provide evidence where available, and identify where the challenge has no merit

scenario.text: A CHO provides a vehicle on day 1. On day 2, the insurer sends a template letter stating: (a) the rate is too high, (b) the claimant should have used public transport, (c) they are investigating liability, and (d) the hire should not exceed 14 days. The claimant is a district nurse who drives to patient homes daily. The repair takes 18 days. Analyse each challenge.
teaching_points: Rate challenge: depends on impecuniosity — must be assessed on the claimant's financial position
Public transport: a district nurse who visits patients at home cannot use public transport for her job — this is a non-starter
Liability investigation: relevant — if liability is not admitted, the CHO takes a risk on recovery; but a district nurse in a clear rear-end collision presents low liability risk
14-day cap: has no legal basis as a unilateral cap; the period depends on facts and reasonable repair time
The learner should identify this as a template letter with no fact-specific engagement and respond accordingly

knowledge_check.question: An insurer's challenge letter states the claimant 'should have used public transport'. The claimant is a community midwife who attends home births. How should the CHO respond?

options.text: Accept the challenge — the insurer has raised a valid point about mitigation
Reject the challenge as unsustainable on the facts — a midwife attending home births cannot use public transport for her professional duties; document the claimant's role and respond clearly
options.explanation: The public transport argument is a standard template challenge. On these facts it has no merit and should be rejected promptly with a clear factual explanation.

options.text: Await the insurer's further evidence before responding
Reduce the hire rate as a goodwill gesture

questions.question: What is the 'credit element' in a CHO rate?
options: A profit margin added by the CHO above the market rate
The cost the CHO incurs by carrying the unpaid hire debt until the claim is settled
An administrative fee for processing the hire agreement
A surcharge applied only to impecunious claimants
questions.explanation: The credit element reflects the CHO's working capital cost of providing vehicles on deferred payment terms. It is the key component that distinguishes CHO rates from spot hire rates and is recoverable where the claimant needed credit.

questions.question: A claimant is impecunious under Lagden v O'Brien. The CHO rate is £85/day and the BHR survey shows spot rates of £40-52/day. What rate is most likely recoverable?
options: £52/day — the highest rate in the BHR survey
£46/day — the average of the BHR survey range
£85/day — the full CHO rate, because an impecunious claimant needed the credit element
£40/day — the lowest available spot rate to minimise cost
questions.explanation: Where the claimant is genuinely impecunious, the full CHO rate is recoverable because the claimant could not have hired at the spot rate. BHR evidence is irrelevant when impecuniosity is established.

questions.question: An insurer produces a BHR survey for a Transit van hire. The survey was obtained 6 weeks after the hire ended, and two of the three companies surveyed had no branch within 40 miles of the hire location. Which weaknesses should the CHO identify?
options: Only the timing is a weakness; the geographic issue is irrelevant
Timing (obtained post-hire) and geography (no local branch presence), both of which undermine the reliability of the survey as a true comparator
The survey is automatically invalid because it was commissioned by the insurer
Only the geographic issue matters; timing of the survey is not relevant
questions.explanation: Both timing and geography are fundamental weaknesses. Rates obtained weeks after the hire may not reflect the market at the time, and companies with no local presence cannot serve as genuine comparators for what the claimant could actually have hired locally.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the court's approach to the impecuniosity threshold?
options: Any claimant earning below £25,000 per year is automatically impecunious
Only claimants on state benefits qualify as impecunious
There is no fixed threshold; the assessment is holistic, considering income, savings, debts, outgoings, and credit availability
A claimant with any available credit on a credit card cannot be impecunious
questions.explanation: Courts have not set a fixed income or savings threshold for impecuniosity. The assessment looks at the whole financial picture: a claimant earning £30,000 with substantial debts may be impecunious, while one earning £18,000 with savings may not be.

questions.question: A claimant self-certifies their impecuniosity by signing a form but provides no supporting financial documents. How much weight will a court likely give this?
options: Full weight — the claimant has confirmed it under their own hand
Full weight, unless the insurer produces contradictory evidence
Limited weight — self-certification without corroborating documentary evidence such as bank statements or payslips is unlikely to be sufficient
No weight at all — impecuniosity can only be proved by a credit reference report
questions.explanation: Self-certification is a starting point, not a conclusion. Courts expect it to be supported by objective evidence such as bank statements, payslips, or other financial documentation.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of digital bank statement technology for impecuniosity evidence?
options: It eliminates the need to prove impecuniosity entirely
It provides faster, less intrusive, and more objective evidence of the claimant's financial position than paper statements
It is the only form of evidence that courts will accept
It automatically determines whether the claimant qualifies as impecunious
questions.explanation: Digital bank statement technology connects directly to the claimant's bank via open banking, producing a categorised summary quickly and with less intrusion. It is not the only acceptable form of evidence, but it offers practical advantages over paper statements.

questions.question: An insurer sends a template challenge letter within 24 hours of hire notification, raising rate, period, need, and public transport arguments without engaging with the specific facts of the claim. How should the CHO treat this letter?
options: Accept all challenges immediately as the insurer has raised valid points
Treat it as tactical until the insurer engages with the specific facts; assess each point on its merits and respond factually
Ignore it entirely as it has no legal significance
Withdraw the hire immediately to avoid further dispute
questions.explanation: Template challenge letters rarely engage with the specific facts and should be treated as tactical. However, they may contain valid points, so each challenge should be assessed on the merits and addressed with evidence where available.

questions.question: True or False: BHR evidence is relevant to the rate recoverable regardless of whether the claimant is impecunious.
questions.explanation: False. BHR evidence is only relevant where the claimant is NOT impecunious. If the claimant is impecunious under Lagden v O'Brien, the full CHO rate is recoverable and BHR evidence is irrelevant because the claimant could not have hired at the spot rate.

questions.question: True or False: Evidence demands for impecuniosity must be proportionate to the value of the rate differential at stake in the claim.
questions.explanation: True. The CPR proportionality principle applies to disclosure in credit hire litigation. Courts have criticised excessive demands and may penalise them in costs. A small rate difference does not justify demanding extensive financial disclosure.

questions.question: True or False: CHO rates must reflect the market rate at the time of settlement rather than at the time of hire.
questions.explanation: False. Rates must reflect the market at the time of hire, not at the time of settlement. The rate is a snapshot of the market when the vehicle was actually hired.

system_prompt_per_section.s01: You are a credit hire training tutor for Section 1 of Module 4: how CHO rates are constructed. The learner is working through a rate dispute scenario — a £85/day CHO rate vs £38-52/day BHR. Guide them to identify that the first question is always impecuniosity; if established, BHR evidence is irrelevant. Keep responses to 2-4 sentences.
system_prompt_per_section.s02: You are a credit hire training tutor for Section 2 of Module 4: BHR evidence. The learner is working through a van hire scenario where the BHR survey has timing, geography and availability weaknesses. Guide them to identify all three weaknesses and explain that the CHO should produce counter-evidence. Keep responses to 2-4 sentences.
system_prompt_per_section.s03: You are a credit hire training tutor for Section 3 of Module 4: impecuniosity evidence. The learner is working through Aisha's scenario — a home care worker facing a disproportionate disclosure demand. Guide them to advise providing proportionate evidence (shorter period, fewer documents) and challenging the scope of the demand. Keep responses to 2-4 sentences.
system_prompt_per_section.s04: You are a credit hire training tutor for Section 4 of Module 4: digital bank statements. The learner is working through a scenario where the insurer refuses to accept digital bank statement evidence. Guide them to challenge the insurer's refusal, assert proportionality, and identify that on the specific financial facts impecuniosity is strongly made out. Reference Forum coverage of this issue. Keep responses to 2-4 sentences.
system_prompt_per_section.s05: You are a credit hire training tutor for Section 5 of Module 4: insurer tactics. The learner is working through a district nurse scenario with a template letter challenging rate, public transport, liability and period. Guide them to identify the template nature of the letter, assess each challenge on the facts, and recognise that the public transport argument is non-existent for a district nurse. Keep responses to 2-4 sentences.
